preamble: A crisp alveolar stop emerges from a dental fricative. This 'stopping' process is one of the most common ways languages simplify their consonant inventories, especially when dental fricatives are perceived as difficult to maintain.

examples.note: High German consonant shift involving the stopping of earlier dental fricatives (shifted to /d/ or /t/).
Frequent realization of the dental fricative as an alveolar stop.

sources: Wells, J. C. (1982). Accents of English.
Waterman, J. T. (1966). A History of the German Language.
